name: Visual Information Flow
summary: A visual signal is routed, interpreted, matched with memory, emotionally tagged, evaluated, and turned into possible action.

steps.title: Eyes receive the signal
steps.description: Sensory input receives the red apple signal.
steps.what: Light reflected by the apple becomes a neural signal.
steps.why: The brain needs a reliable entry point before it can interpret the outside world.
steps.analogy: Like a camera sensor capturing raw pixels before software can recognize an image.

steps.title: Thalamus routes the signal
steps.description: The thalamus directs visual data toward visual processing.
steps.what: The signal is relayed to the visual cortex.
steps.why: Routing prevents the brain from treating every signal the same way.
steps.analogy: Like a switchboard sending the right call to the right department.

steps.title: Visual cortex detects features
steps.description: Shape, color, edge, and object features are processed.
steps.what: The visual cortex identifies red color, rounded shape, and object boundaries.
steps.why: Meaning begins with structured features, not raw light.
steps.analogy: Like an image recognition system finding color, edges, and shape.

steps.title: Hippocampus checks memory
steps.description: Memory systems match the object with the concept apple.
steps.what: The hippocampus connects the current image with stored knowledge.
steps.why: Recognition depends on linking new input with prior experience.
steps.analogy: Like searching a personal library for a matching entry.

steps.title: Amygdala adds context
steps.description: The object is tagged as safe, familiar, and possibly rewarding.
steps.what: Emotional context influences whether the object matters right now.
steps.why: The brain prioritizes signals with emotional or survival value.
steps.analogy: Like a priority label on an incoming message.

steps.title: Prefrontal cortex decides
steps.description: Goals, hunger, context, and memory are compared.
steps.what: Decision systems evaluate whether to eat, ignore, or save the apple.
steps.why: Flexible behavior requires weighing evidence against goals.
steps.analogy: Like a project manager comparing options before assigning work.

steps.title: Motor cortex prepares action
steps.description: A movement plan is prepared if action is chosen.
steps.what: Motor areas prepare reaching, grasping, or no movement.
steps.why: Decisions need translation into body-ready commands.
steps.analogy: Like turning a decision into instructions for a robotic arm.

name: Memory Storage Flow
summary: Attention and hippocampal encoding help a new idea become a stronger long-term association.

steps.title: Concept enters attention
steps.description: Sensory input receives a new concept.
steps.what: Words, images, or examples enter working awareness.
steps.why: Learning starts when information becomes available to attention.
steps.analogy: Like opening a file before editing it.

steps.title: Attention focuses
steps.description: The prefrontal cortex filters distraction and holds the idea active.
steps.what: Attention stabilizes the new concept long enough to encode it.
steps.why: Unattended information is less likely to become durable memory.
steps.analogy: Like pinning an important note on the screen.

steps.title: Hippocampus encodes
steps.description: The hippocampus binds details into a retrievable pattern.
steps.what: Different details are linked into a coherent memory trace.
steps.why: Binding lets the brain reconstruct the concept later.
steps.analogy: Like creating an index card that points to connected pages.

steps.title: Associations strengthen
steps.description: Long-term networks store and connect the concept.
steps.what: Distributed cortical networks add meaning and associations.
steps.why: Durable knowledge is stored across connected patterns.
steps.analogy: Like adding backlinks across a knowledge base.

steps.title: Repetition reinforces
steps.description: Repeated activation strengthens the pathway.
steps.what: Each recall or practice pass makes the route easier to activate.
steps.why: Memory strength grows through repeated coordinated firing.
steps.analogy: Like a trail becoming clearer the more people walk it.

name: Memory Retrieval Flow
summary: A goal-driven search reactivates stored associations and returns a name with context.

steps.title: PFC asks for memory
steps.description: The prefrontal cortex defines the retrieval goal.
steps.what: The brain asks: what is this person's name?
steps.why: Retrieval is guided by current goals and cues.
steps.analogy: Like typing a search query.

steps.title: Hippocampus searches traces
steps.description: Related memory traces are cued and compared.
steps.what: Face, place, voice, and context cues narrow the search.
steps.why: Memories are reconstructed from related cues.
steps.analogy: Like finding a contact by photo, location, and notes.

steps.title: Stored information reactivates
steps.description: The long-term network reactivates the associated name.
steps.what: Distributed knowledge patterns become active again.
steps.why: Remembering means reactivating a stored pattern, not replaying a video.
steps.analogy: Like restoring a saved workspace.

steps.title: Emotion adds context
steps.description: Familiarity or social emotion can shape confidence.
steps.what: The emotional system marks whether the memory feels familiar.
steps.why: Emotional tags can help prioritize and validate recall.
steps.analogy: Like a confidence marker beside a search result.

steps.title: Retrieved memory returns
steps.description: The prefrontal cortex receives and uses the remembered name.
steps.what: The recalled name enters conscious use.
steps.why: A memory becomes useful when it can guide behavior.
steps.analogy: Like selecting the right contact and using it in a message.

name: Decision-Making Flow
scenario: User decides whether to cross a road.
summary: Sensory evidence, memory, emotion, and logic converge into a movement decision.

steps.title: Senses detect traffic
steps.description: Eyes and ears detect cars, sound, and movement.
steps.what: The brain receives visual and auditory road cues.
steps.why: Decisions need current evidence from the environment.
steps.analogy: Like sensors feeding a driving-assistance system.

steps.title: Thalamus routes cues
steps.description: The thalamus routes traffic information to sensory cortices.
steps.what: Visual and auditory streams are sent to specialized processors.
steps.why: Fast routing helps the brain separate what it sees from what it hears.
steps.analogy: Like a traffic controller splitting lanes.

steps.title: Visual motion is processed
steps.description: The visual cortex estimates car position and movement.
steps.what: Motion, distance, and direction are analyzed.
steps.why: Crossing safely depends on predicting moving objects.
steps.analogy: Like a motion tracker estimating trajectory.

steps.title: Sound is interpreted
steps.description: The auditory cortex detects horns and engine direction.
steps.what: Sound cues add urgency and direction information.
steps.why: Hearing can reveal threats outside direct view.
steps.analogy: Like an alert system catching off-screen signals.

steps.title: Memory retrieves road rules
steps.description: The hippocampus retrieves past knowledge and experiences.
steps.what: Stored rules and memories shape the interpretation.
steps.why: Past learning helps predict danger and appropriate action.
steps.analogy: Like consulting previous case examples.

steps.title: Risk is evaluated
steps.description: The amygdala adds danger and urgency signals.
steps.what: Potential threat is marked as low, uncertain, or high.
steps.why: Emotion helps prioritize safety-relevant decisions.
steps.analogy: Like a warning light on a dashboard.

steps.title: Logic compares options
steps.description: The prefrontal cortex weighs risk, goal, timing, and rules.
steps.what: The brain chooses wait, cross, or gather more evidence.
steps.why: Controlled decisions balance urgency with long-term goals.
steps.analogy: Like a planner comparing safety, time, and objective.

steps.title: Action signal is sent
steps.description: The motor cortex prepares the final movement.
steps.what: Movement systems prepare stepping forward or staying still.
steps.why: A decision must become a concrete body command.
steps.analogy: Like sending final instructions to an actuator.
